mnemonics.title: Page Replacement Algorithms
mnemonics.content:
FIFO - FIRST IN FIRST OUT (oldest page leaves first)
LRU - LEAST RECENTLY USED (forgotten longest is replaced
OPTIMAL - Looking forward to find best choice
SECOND CHANCE - Give pages a second chance if recently used

mnemonics.title: Memory Fragmentation Types
mnemonics.content:
INTERNAL: INSIDE allocated block (Fixed-size waste)
EXTERNAL: External holes between blocks (Scattered free space)
COMPACTION: COMPresses to bring blocks together (fixes external)

mnemonics.title: Virtual Memory Benefits
mnemonics.content:
VIRTUAL MEMORY provides:
V - Very large address space
I - Isolation between processes
R - Reduced I/O (demand loading)
T - Thrashing prevention (if sized correctly)
U - Utilization of physical memory improved
A - Allocation flexibility
L - Larger programs can run
M - Memory protection provided

acronyms.usage: Hardware component that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses
Hardware cache that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations
Page replacement algorithm that removes oldest page in memory
Page replacement algorithm that removes least recently accessed page
Page replacement algorithm that removes least recently accessed page
Executes instructions and generates logical memory addresses

visualTips.visualization: Think of virtual address as a book (page number = chapter, offset = page number within chapter). Physical address is where each chapter's pages are actually stored on library shelves (frame = shelf location). Page table is like a library catalog showing which shelf contains each chapter's pages.

Imagine a library where books (pages) you haven't read yet are stored in the basement (disk). A page fault is like requesting a book not on your desk - you must go get it from the basement (slow), bring it to your desk (frame), update your reading list (page table), then continue reading.

Think of thrashing as someone constantly shuffling papers on a tiny desk - they spend more time organizing papers than actually reading them. More desk space (memory) or fewer tasks (processes) would help.
Your working set is like the books currently open on your desk - the ones you're actively using. Close ones you haven't touched in a while to make room for new books.

analogies.analogy: Virtual memory is like a library's book reservation system. You can request any book in the library (large virtual space), but only have a few on your desk at a time (physical RAM). The librarian (OS) brings books from the stacks (disk) when you need them.

Paging is like having a photo album where each page (frame) holds the same fixed number of pictures (bytes). Your master album (virtual memory) has many more pages than your physical photo book (RAM), and you swap pages as needed.

Segmentation is like organizing a textbook into logical sections: Chapter 1 (code), Chapter 2 (data), Chapter 3 (stack). Each chapter can be different lengths and has different access rules (read-only for code, read-write for data).

A page table is like a map in a large shopping mall. The map shows which floor (frame) each store (virtual page) is located on. When you want to visit a specific store, you look at the map to find its floor, then navigate there.
TLB is like keeping a sticky note of your frequently visited stores in the mall. Instead of checking the map every time, you just look at your sticky note for quick directions to common destinations.

Internal fragmentation is like buying a pre-packed gift box that's slightly too big - you have wasted space inside. External fragmentation is like having small coins scattered in different pockets - you can't buy something that needs a bill even though you have enough total money.
